title: 💥 SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE
content:
Classic clue: "Worst headache of my life" (Thunderclap).

• Sudden onset.
• Neck stiffness (Meningismus).
• Often due to Berry Aneurysm rupture.

InvestigationWhy?
CT HeadFirst line (detects blood)
Lumbar PunctureIf CT is negative (Xanthochromia)

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive test if CT is negative for SAH?

title: 🧠 EPIDURAL HEMATOMA
content:
Classic history: Trauma → Lucid interval (feels fine) → Rapid decline.

• Artery: Middle Meningeal Artery.
• Shape: Lens-shaped (Biconvex).

quiz.q: Which artery is typically ruptured in Epidural Hematoma?

title: 🔪 APPENDICITIS
content:
Pain Pattern: Periumbilical → migrating to McBurney's point (RLQ).

• Signs: Rovsing, Psoas, Obturator signs.
• Tx: Appendectomy.

SignAction
ReboundPeritonitis sign
AnorexiaVery common feature

quiz.q: Where does the pain migrate in acute appendicitis?
quiz.a: From the periumbilical area to the Right Lower Quadrant (RLQ).

title: 🍬 DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS (DKA)
content:
Triad: Hyperglycemia + Ketosis + Acidosis.

• Dx: Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath.
• Tx: SIPS (Saline, Insulin, Potassium, Search for trigger).

Pro Tip: Never start Insulin if Potassium is < 3.3!

quiz.q: What is the classic breathing pattern in DKA?

title: 🦠 NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
content:
Critical Clue: Pain out of proportion to physical findings.

• Rapid spread.
• Systemic toxicity (Fever, hypotension).
• Next Step: Emergency Surgical Debridement (Don't wait for imaging!).

quiz.q: Do you wait for MRI to diagnose Necrotizing Fasciitis?
quiz.a: NO! It's a clinical diagnosis; proceed immediately to surgical debridement.

title: 🧠 BACTERIAL MENINGITIS
content:
Time is neurons! Empiric therapy must cover S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis.

Age / RiskEmpiric Antibiotics
18 - 50 yearsCeftriaxone + Vancomycin
> 50 yearsCeftriaxone + Vanco + Ampicillin

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, if you suspect meningitis, the FIRST thing you do is give Dexamethasone (before or with the first dose of antibiotics) to prevent hearing loss!

quiz.q: Why is Ampicillin added for patients over 50?

title: 👶 DEVELOPMENTAL MILESTONES (1 YEAR)
content:
The 'Rule of Ones' for a 12-month-old:

• Gross Motor: Stands alone, takes first steps.
• Fine Motor: Pincer grasp (picks up a raisin).
• Language: Says 1-3 words (Mama/Dada).
• Social: Plays 'Pat-a-cake'.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If a child is not walking by 18 months, it is a mandatory red flag for referral!

quiz.q: When does a child usually start using a pincer grasp?

title: 🤰 ECTOPIC PREGNANCY
content:
Key Triad: Amenorrhea + Abdominal Pain + Vaginal Bleeding.

Management Algorithm:
1. Stable + β-hCG < 5000: Consider Methotrexate.
2. Unstable or Ruptured: Emergency Laparoscopy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Unstable (Low BP, High HR), the next step is NOT an ultrasound. It is Emergency Laparotomy/Surgery!

quiz.q: What is the most common site for an ectopic pregnancy?
quiz.a: The Ampulla of the Fallopian tube.

title: 🥵 ACUTE PANCREATITIS
content:
Diagnosis requires 2 out of 3:
1. Epigastric pain (radiates to back).
2. Lipase > 3x upper limit.
3. Characteristic imaging (CT).

MarkerClinical Use
LipaseBest for diagnosis (stays high longer)
ALT > 150Suggests Gallstone etiology

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the diagnosis is clear (Pain + High Lipase), CT scan is NOT required in the first 48 hours and is a wrong answer!

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of acute pancreatitis in Canada?

title: 🩸 HEPARIN-INDUCED THROMBOCYTOPENIA (HIT)
content:
Paradox: Low Platelets but HIGH risk of Clots (Thrombosis).

• Timing: 5-10 days after starting Heparin.
• Diagnosis: 4T Score (Timing, Thrombocytopenia, Thrombosis, oTher causes).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you suspect HIT, STOP Heparin immediately and start a Non-heparin anticoagulant (like Argatroban or Danaparoid). Do NOT give Platelets!

quiz.q: What is the definitive test for HIT?

title: 🌪️ DELIRIUM VS DEMENTIA
content:
The most common geriatric consult in the ED.

FeatureDeliriumDementia
OnsetAcute / AbruptGradual / Years
ConsciousnessFluctuatingIntact (early)
AttentionImpairedNormal (early)

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step in a patient with new Delirium is NOT a head CT. It is to look for Infection (UTI), Meds, or Electrolyte imbalance!

quiz.q: What is the hallmark clinical feature of Delirium?

title: 🔪 ACUTE APPENDICITIS
content:
Sequence: Anorexia → Periumbilical Pain → Vomiting → RLQ Pain.


⚠️ Exam Trap: In children and pregnant women, Ultrasound is the first imaging. In non-pregnant adults, CT with contrast is the gold standard.

quiz.a: Pain in the RLQ when pressing on the LLQ.

title: ⚖️ MATURE MINOR DOCTRINE
content:
In Canada (except Quebec), there is no fixed age for consent.

• A minor can consent if they understand the nature, risks, and benefits of the treatment.
• Sexual Health/Contraception: Usually always confidential for minors.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a 14-year-old is 'mature' and refuses a blood transfusion that their parents want, you must follow the minor's decision (CMPA rules).

quiz.q: At what age is a person considered an adult for medical consent in Quebec?
